text: What is chunking? - The process of splitting large documents into smaller, manageable pieces before processing or embedding. Why is chunking important? - It improves retrieval accuracy by ensuring only relevant parts of a document are retrieved. Why not embed full documents?

Why not embed full documents? - Large documents dilute meaning and reduce search precision. What is chunk size? - The length of each split piece, usually measured in tokens or characters. How does chunk size affect performance?

How does chunk size affect performance? - Smaller chunks improve precision, larger chunks provide more context. What is ideal chunk size? - Depends on use case, commonly 200-500 tokens for LLM-based systems. What is chunk overlap?
What is chunk overlap? - Repeating some content between chunks to preserve context. Why use overlap? - Prevents loss of important information at chunk boundaries. What happens without overlap?

What happens without overlap? - Important context may be split and lost during retrieval. What is sliding window chunking? - Moving window approach with overlap between chunks. What is fixed-size chunking?

What is fixed-size chunking? - Splitting text into equal-sized pieces regardless of meaning. What is semantic chunking? - Splitting text based on meaning, like sentences or paragraphs. Why is semantic chunking better?

Why is semantic chunking better? - Preserves natural language structure and improves retrieval quality. What is sentence-based chunking? - Splitting text using sentence boundaries. What is paragraph-based chunking?
What is paragraph-based chunking? - Splitting text into paragraphs. What is token-based chunking? - Splitting based on token count (used in LLM pipelines). What is recursive chunking?
What is recursive chunking? - Splitting text hierarchically using rules (used in LangChain). Why recursive chunking useful? - Maintains structure while ensuring size limits. What is chunking in RAG?

What is chunking in RAG? - Preparing documents into smaller parts before embedding and storing. How chunking affects embeddings? - Smaller chunks produce more focused and meaningful vectors. What is chunk metadata?

What is chunk metadata? - Information like source, page number, or section stored with each chunk. Why metadata important? - Helps trace back retrieved results to original documents. What is context window?
What is context window? - Maximum tokens an LLM can process at once. Why chunking needed for context window? - Ensures retrieved text fits within model limits. What is chunk retrieval?
What is chunk retrieval? - Fetching relevant chunks based on query similarity. What is top-k chunk retrieval? - Retrieving the most relevant k chunks. What is chunk redundancy?
What is chunk redundancy? - Repeated information across chunks due to overlap. Is redundancy bad? - Slight redundancy is useful for preserving context. What is chunk granularity?
What is chunk granularity? - Level of detail in chunking (fine vs coarse). What is fine-grained chunking? - Smaller chunks, more precise retrieval. What is coarse-grained chunking?
What is coarse-grained chunking? - Larger chunks, more context but less precision. What is chunk indexing? - Storing chunk embeddings in vector database. What is chunk embedding pipeline?
What is chunk embedding pipeline? - Process: clean  split  embed  store. What is preprocessing before chunking? - Cleaning text, removing noise, formatting. What is chunk quality?
What is chunk quality? - How meaningful and self-contained a chunk is. Why chunk quality matters? - Better chunks lead to better retrieval and answers. What is chunk boundary problem?
What is chunk boundary problem? - Important information split between chunks. How to solve boundary problem? - Use overlap or semantic chunking. What is dynamic chunking?
What is dynamic chunking? - Adjusting chunk size based on content. What is adaptive chunking? - Changing chunk strategy depending on document type. What is chunking for PDFs?
What is chunking for PDFs? - Splitting extracted text page-wise or section-wise. What is chunking for JSON? - Splitting structured data into logical parts. What is chunking for code?
What is chunking for code? - Splitting based on functions or logical blocks. What is chunk size vs embedding cost? - Smaller chunks increase number of embeddings and cost. What is chunking tradeoff?

What is chunking tradeoff? - Balance between context (large chunks) and precision (small chunks). What is hierarchical chunking? - Splitting into sections  paragraphs  sentences. What is chunk retrieval failure?

What is chunk retrieval failure? - When relevant chunk is not retrieved. Why chunking impacts RAG performance? - Poor chunking leads to irrelevant retrieval and wrong answers. What is best practice for chunking?
What is best practice for chunking? - Use semantic  overlap  proper size. What is chunking evaluation? - Testing retrieval quality with different chunk strategies. What is chunking in production RAG?
What is chunking in production RAG? - Carefully tuned to balance speed, cost, and accuracy. Why chunking is critical in RAG? - It directly affects retrieval quality, which impacts final LLM output.

What are embeddings? - Numerical vector representations of data that capture semantic meaning. Why are embeddings important? - They convert human language into machine- understandable format. What is semantic meaning in embeddings?
What is semantic meaning in embeddings? - Similar words have similar vector representations. Example of similar embeddings? - king and queen are close in vector space. What is vector space?
What is vector space? - Mathematical space where embeddings are represented as points. What is embedding dimension? - Number of values in a vector (e.g., 768). Higher dimension means what?
Higher dimension means what? - More information but higher computation cost. What is cosine similarity? - Measures similarity between vectors based on angle. Why use cosine similarity?
Why use cosine similarity? - It focuses on meaning rather than magnitude. Range of cosine similarity? - From -1 to 1. What does similarity  1 mean?
What does similarity  1 mean? - Vectors are highly similar in meaning. What is an embedding model? - A model that converts data into vectors. Example embedding models?
Example embedding models? - Sentence Transformers, OpenAI embeddings. What is Word2Vec? - Model that learns word meaning from context. CBOW vs Skip-gram?
CBOW vs Skip-gram? - CBOW predicts word; Skip-gram predicts context. What is GloVe? - Uses global word co-occurrence to learn embeddings. Static vs contextual embeddings?
Static vs contextual embeddings? - Static same always; contextual depends on sentence. Example of contextual embeddings? - BERT embeddings. Why contextual embeddings better?
Why contextual embeddings better? - Capture different meanings of same word. What is sentence embedding? - Vector representing full sentence meaning. What is document embedding?
What is document embedding? - Vector representing entire document. What is embedding in RAG? - Converts queries and docs into vectors for retrieval. Why embeddings used in RAG?
Why embeddings used in RAG? - Enables semantic search instead of keyword matching. What is semantic search? - Search based on meaning, not exact words. Example of semantic search?
Example of semantic search? - car matching vehicle. What is vector database? - Database that stores embeddings. Example vector DBs?
Example vector DBs? - FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate. What is indexing in vector DB? - Organizing vectors for fast retrieval. What is nearest neighbor search?
What is nearest neighbor search? - Finding most similar vectors. What is ANN? - Approximate nearest neighbor for faster search. Why ANN used?
Why ANN used? - Speeds up search in large datasets. What is embedding drift? - Change in embedding meaning over time. What is normalization in embeddings?
What is normalization in embeddings? - Scaling vectors to unit length. Why normalize embeddings? - Improves similarity comparison. What is dot product similarity?
What is dot product similarity? - Measures similarity using vector multiplication. Embedding vs one-hot encoding? - Embeddings are dense; one-hot is sparse. What is sparse vs dense vector?
What is sparse vs dense vector? - Sparse has many zeros; dense has meaningful values. What is dimensionality reduction? - Reducing vector size while keeping meaning. Example method?
Example method? - PCA. What is embedding clustering? - Grouping similar vectors together. Use of clustering?
Use of clustering? - Topic detection and grouping similar data. What is similarity threshold? - Minimum score to consider vectors similar. What is cross-encoder vs bi-encoder?
What is cross-encoder vs bi-encoder? - Cross compares pairs; bi encodes separately. Why bi-encoder used in RAG? - Faster retrieval performance. What is embedding cache?
What is embedding cache? - Storing embeddings to avoid recomputation. What is embedding pipeline? - Process: clean  chunk  embed  store. What is chunking before embedding?

What is chunking before embedding? - Splitting large text into smaller, manageable parts so each piece can be embedded effectively. Why is chunking needed? - It improves retrieval accuracy by ensuring relevant and focused information is retrieved instead of large noisy text. What is multilingual embedding?

What is multilingual embedding? - Embeddings that represent multiple languages in a shared vector space, allowing cross-language understanding. Why are embeddings core to AILLMRAG? - They convert data into meaningful vectors, enabling semantic search, retrieval, and intelligent generation.

What is an LLM? - A Large Language Model trained on massive text data to understand and generate human-like language. Why are LLMs powerful? - They capture language patterns, context, and reasoning from large-scale training. What is NLP?
What is NLP? - Field of AI focused on understanding and generating human language. What is a language model? - Model that predicts the next wordtoken in a sequence. What is pretraining?
What is pretraining? - Training on large general datasets to learn language patterns. What is fine-tuning? - Training on specific data to adapt model behavior. What is generative AI?
What is generative AI? - AI that creates new content like text, images, or code. What is autoregressive model? - Generates text one token at a time. What is token?
What is token? - Smallest unit of text processed by model. What is context in LLM? - Previous tokens used to generate next output. What is tokenization?
What is tokenization? - Splitting text into smaller units like words or subwords. Why tokenization needed? - Models cannot process raw text directly. What is subword tokenization?
What is subword tokenization? - Breaking words into smaller meaningful parts. What is BPE? - Byte Pair Encoding method for tokenization. What is WordPiece?
What is WordPiece? - Tokenization method used in BERT. What is tokenizer vocabulary? - Set of tokens known to the model. What is OOV token?
What is OOV token? - Out-of-vocabulary token not recognized. Why token count important? - Affects cost and context window. What is special token?
What is special token? - Tokens like CLS, SEP, or EOS. What is padding? - Adding tokens to match input length. What is embedding?
What is embedding? - Vector representation of tokens. Why embeddings used? - Convert text into numerical format. What is positional encoding?
What is positional encoding? - Adds order information to tokens. Why positional encoding needed? - Transformers lack sequence awareness. What is embedding layer?
What is embedding layer? - First layer mapping tokens to vectors. What is contextual embedding? - Meaning depends on surrounding words. What is static embedding?
What is static embedding? - Same vector regardless of context. Why contextual embeddings better? - Capture dynamic meanings. What is hidden state?
What is hidden state? - Internal representation of token in model. What is representation learning? - Learning useful data features automatically. What is transformer architecture?

What is transformer architecture? - Deep learning model using attention mechanism. Why transformers replaced RNNs? - Better parallelism and long-range dependency handling. What is attention mechanism?
What is attention mechanism? - Focus on relevant parts of input. What is self-attention? - Tokens attend to each other. What are Q, K, V?
What are Q, K, V? - Query, Key, Value matrices used in attention. What is attention score? - Importance of one token to another. What is softmax in attention?
What is softmax in attention? - Converts scores into probabilities. What is multi-head attention? - Multiple attention layers capturing different patterns. What is feedforward layer?
What is feedforward layer? - Fully connected layer after attention. What is layer normalization? - Stabilizes training. What is encoder?
What is encoder? - Processes input and builds representation. What is decoder? - Generates output sequence. What is encoder-only model?
What is encoder-only model? - Used for understanding tasks (e.g., BERT). What is decoder-only model? - Used for generation (e.g., GPT). What is encoder-decoder model?
What is encoder-decoder model? - Used for translation tasks. Why GPT is decoder-only? - Designed for text generation. Why BERT is encoder-only?
Why BERT is encoder-only? - Designed for understanding context. What is masked language model? - Predict missing tokens (BERT). What is causal language model?
What is causal language model? - Predict next token (GPT). What is seq2seq model? - Maps input sequence to output sequence. What is training objective?
What is training objective? - Minimize prediction error. What is loss function? - Measures difference between prediction and actual. What is cross-entropy loss?
What is cross-entropy loss? - Common loss for language models. What is gradient descent? - Optimization method to update weights. What is backpropagation?
What is backpropagation? - Computes gradients for updates. What is optimizer? - Algorithm like Adam for training. What is learning rate?
What is learning rate? - Step size for updates. What is epoch? - One full pass of dataset. What is batch size?
What is batch size? - Number of samples per update. What is overfitting? - Model memorizes training data. What is inference?
What is inference? - Using trained model to generate output. What is decoding? - Process of generating tokens. What is greedy decoding?
What is greedy decoding? - Picks highest probability token. What is beam search? - Explores multiple sequences. What is temperature?
What is temperature? - Controls randomness in output. What is top-k sampling? - Limits selection to top k tokens. What is top-p sampling?
What is top-p sampling? - Chooses tokens based on cumulative probability. Why sampling needed? - To make output more diverse. What is deterministic output?
What is deterministic output? - Same input gives same output. What is stochastic output? - Output varies due to randomness.
What is prompt? - Input given to LLM. What is prompt engineering? - Designing prompts for better output. Why prompts important?
Why prompts important? - LLM behavior depends on input. What is system prompt? - Instructions guiding model behavior. What is few-shot prompting?
What is few-shot prompting? - Providing examples. What is zero-shot prompting? - No examples given. What is chain-of-thought?
What is chain-of-thought? - Encouraging step-by-step reasoning. What is role prompting? - Assigning role like expert. What is instruction prompt?
What is instruction prompt? - Direct task instruction. What is prompt template? - Structured reusable format. What is hallucination?
What is hallucination? - Confident but incorrect output. Why hallucination happens? - Model predicts patterns, not facts. What is bias in LLM?
What is bias in LLM? - Learned bias from training data. What is knowledge cutoff? - Model knowledge limited to training data. What is context window limit?
What is context window limit? - Maximum tokens model can process. What is token cost? - Cost per inputoutput token. What is latency?
What is latency? - Response time. What is model size issue? - Large models require high compute. What is lack of reasoning?
What is lack of reasoning? - Limited logical consistency sometimes. What is data privacy risk? - Sensitive data exposure. What is LLM role in RAG?
What is LLM role in RAG? - Generates answer using retrieved context. Why combine LLM with RAG? - Improves factual accuracy. What is context injection?
What is context injection? - Adding retrieved data to prompt. What is grounded response? - Answer based on real data. What is retrieval augmentation?
What is retrieval augmentation? - Enhancing LLM with external knowledge. What is hallucination reduction in RAG? - Using real documents. What is prompt  context format?
What is prompt  context format? - Query  retrieved chunks. What is knowledge update via RAG? - No retraining needed. What is dynamic knowledge?
What is dynamic knowledge? - Real-time data usage. What is RAG vs fine-tuning? - RAG is flexible, fine-tuning is static. What is fine-tuning?
What is fine-tuning? - Training model on custom data. What is instruction tuning? - Training to follow instructions. What is RLHF?
What is RLHF? - Reinforcement learning from human feedback. What is alignment? - Making model safe and useful. What is LoRA?
What is LoRA? - Efficient fine-tuning technique. What is quantization? - Reducing model size. What is distillation?
What is distillation? - Creating smaller models. What is multi-modal LLM? - Handles text, images, audio. What is agentic LLM?
What is agentic LLM? - Uses tools and reasoning. What is tool calling? - LLM interacts with APIstools. What is prompt injection?
What is prompt injection? - Malicious input manipulating model. What are guardrails? - Safety constraints on outputs. What is moderation?
What is moderation? - Filtering harmful content. What is logging? - Tracking usage and outputs. What is monitoring?
What is monitoring? - Observing system behavior. What is scaling? - Handling large traffic. What is deployment?
What is deployment? - Running model in production. What is API usage? - Accessing LLM via API. What is cost optimization?
What is cost optimization? - Reducing token usage. What is caching? - Storing repeated responses. Where LLM used?
Where LLM used? - Chatbots, coding assistants, search, translation. What is coding assistant? - Helps generate code. What is chatbot?
What is chatbot? - Conversational AI system. What is summarization? - Condensing long text. What is translation?
What is translation? - Converting between languages. What is sentiment analysis? - Detecting emotions. What is QA system?
What is QA system? - Answering questions from text. What is content generation? - Writing articles, emails. What is personalization?
What is personalization? - Custom responses. What is enterprise LLM? - Used with private company data.

What is RAG? - A system that combines retrieval of external data with LLM generation to produce accurate, grounded responses. Why RAG is needed? - LLMs have limited knowledge and can hallucinate; RAG adds real data. What does RAG solve?

What does RAG solve? - Reduces hallucination and enables use of private or updated data. What are core components of RAG? - Data ingestion, chunking, embeddings, vector DB, retrieval, and generation. What is retrieval in RAG?
What is retrieval in RAG? - Fetching relevant documents using similarity search. What is generation in RAG? - LLM generating answer using retrieved context. What is knowledge base in RAG?
What is knowledge base in RAG? - Collection of documents stored for retrieval. What is grounding in RAG? - Ensuring answers are based on retrieved data. What is hallucination?
What is hallucination? - LLM generating incorrect but confident answers. How RAG reduces hallucination? - By providing real context from data. What is data ingestion?
What is data ingestion? - Loading raw data (PDF, text, JSON) into pipeline. Why data cleaning needed? - Removes noise and improves embedding quality. What is preprocessing?
What is preprocessing? - Formatting and preparing data before chunking. What formats used in RAG? - PDFs, TXT, JSON, HTML, databases. What is document loader?
What is document loader? - Tool to read and extract data (e.g., LangChain loaders). What is text extraction? - Converting PDF or files into plain text. What is noisy data?
What is noisy data? - Irrelevant or messy content affecting retrieval. Why remove special characters? - Improves embedding consistency. What is deduplication?
What is deduplication? - Removing repeated data. Why structured data helpful? - Easier to chunk and retrieve. What is chunking?
What is chunking? - Splitting documents into smaller parts. Why chunking important? - Improves retrieval precision. What is chunk size?
What is chunk size? - Number of tokens per chunk. What is overlap? - Shared content between chunks. Why overlap used?
Why overlap used? - Maintains context continuity. What is semantic chunking? - Splitting based on meaning. What is fixed chunking?
What is fixed chunking? - Equal size splitting. What is recursive chunking? - Hierarchical splitting method. What is chunk metadata?
What is chunk metadata? - Info like source, page, section. What is chunk quality? - How meaningful and self-contained chunk is. What are embeddings?
What are embeddings? - Vector representations of text. Why embeddings needed? - Enable semantic similarity search. What is embedding model?
What is embedding model? - Converts text into vectors. What is vector dimension? - Size of embedding vector. What is semantic similarity?
What is semantic similarity? - Similar meaning  similar vectors. What is cosine similarity? - Measures angle similarity between vectors. Why normalize embeddings?
Why normalize embeddings? - Improves comparison accuracy. What is sentence embedding? - Vector of full sentence. What is document embedding?
What is document embedding? - Vector of full document. What is multilingual embedding? - Supports multiple languages. What is vector DB?
What is vector DB? - Stores embeddings for fast retrieval. Why vector DB used? - Enables semantic search. What is FAISS?
What is FAISS? - Local vector search library. What is Pinecone? - Managed cloud vector DB. What is indexing?
What is indexing? - Organizing vectors for fast search. What is ANN? - Approximate nearest neighbor search. Why ANN used?
Why ANN used? - Faster search with large data. What is top-k retrieval? - Getting most similar k results. What is metadata filtering?
What is metadata filtering? - Filtering results using extra info. What is hybrid search? - Combining keyword  vector search.
What is retriever? - Component that fetches relevant chunks. What is similarity search? - Finding closest vectors. What is query embedding?
What is query embedding? - Converting user query to vector. What is retrieval accuracy? - How relevant retrieved chunks are. What is recall?
What is recall? - Ability to retrieve all relevant results. What is precision? - Correctness of retrieved results. What is re-ranking?
What is re-ranking? - Reordering results for better relevance. What is cross-encoder? - Model comparing query and result together. Why re-ranking needed?
Why re-ranking needed? - Improves final answer quality. What is retrieval failure? - When relevant data is not retrieved. What is prompt in RAG?
What is prompt in RAG? - Input to LLM including query  context. What is context injection? - Adding retrieved data into prompt. Why prompt important?
Why prompt important? - Directly affects output quality. What is system prompt? - Instructions guiding model behavior. What is temperature?
What is temperature? - Controls randomness of output. What is max tokens? - Limits output length. What is prompt template?
What is prompt template? - Structured format for inputs. What is instruction tuning? - Training model to follow instructions. What is grounded response?
What is grounded response? - Answer based on retrieved data. What is hallucination control? - Limiting incorrect outputs. What is RAG pipeline?
What is RAG pipeline? - End-to-end flow from data to answer. What is pipeline order? - Ingest  chunk  embed  store  retrieve  generate. Why pipeline modular?
Why pipeline modular? - Each step can be optimized separately. What is latency? - Time taken for response. What affects latency?
What affects latency? - Retrieval  LLM speed. What is batch processing? - Handling multiple inputs together. What is real-time RAG?
What is real-time RAG? - Instant query response system. What is offline indexing? - Preprocessing data before queries. What is online retrieval?
What is online retrieval? - Fetching data during query time. What is caching? - Storing results to reduce computation. What is RAG evaluation?
What is RAG evaluation? - Measuring system performance. What is answer accuracy? - Correctness of output. What is faithfulness?
What is faithfulness? - Answer matches retrieved context. What is context relevance? - Quality of retrieved chunks. What is BLEU score?
What is BLEU score? - Measures text similarity. What is ROUGE score? - Measures overlap with reference. What is human evaluation?
What is human evaluation? - Manual checking of outputs. What is hallucination rate? - Frequency of incorrect answers. What is latency evaluation?
What is latency evaluation? - Response time measurement. What is AB testing? - Comparing different RAG setups. What is multi-hop RAG?
What is multi-hop RAG? - Retrieval across multiple steps. What is conversational RAG? - Handles chat history. What is memory in RAG?
What is memory in RAG? - Stores past interactions. What is agentic RAG? - Uses agents for reasoning. What is tool calling?
What is tool calling? - LLM using external tools. What is hybrid RAG? - Combines multiple retrieval methods. What is graph RAG?
What is graph RAG? - Uses knowledge graphs. What is structured RAG? - Works with structured data. What is adaptive RAG?
What is adaptive RAG? - Adjusts retrieval dynamically. What is retrieval augmentation? - Enhancing context with external sources. What is prompt injection?
What is prompt injection? - Malicious input manipulating model. Why security important? - Protects system from misuse. What are guardrails?
What are guardrails? - Controls for safe output. What is data privacy? - Protecting sensitive information. What is access control?
What is access control? - Restricting data access. What is logging? - Tracking system activity. What is monitoring?
What is monitoring? - Observing system performance. What is scaling? - Handling large usersdata. What is cost optimization?
What is cost optimization? - Reducing API usage. What is deployment? - Running RAG system in production. Where is RAG used?
Where is RAG used? - Chatbots, search engines, assistants. Why companies use RAG? - Use private  real-time data. What is enterprise RAG?
What is enterprise RAG? - RAG with company data. What is domain-specific RAG? - Specialized knowledge system. What is legal RAG?
What is legal RAG? - Used for legal documents. What is medical RAG? - Used for healthcare data. What is customer support RAG?
What is customer support RAG? - Automated support systems. What is documentation RAG? - Answering from docs. What is coding RAG?
What is coding RAG? - Helps developers with code. What is best practice in RAG? - Good chunking  embeddings  retrieval tuning.

What is self-attention? A mechanism where each token attends to all other tokens in the same sequence to capture relationships. Why self-attention is important? It allows the model to understand context and dependencies between words regardless of distance. What problem does self-attention solve?

What problem does self-attention solve? It handles long-range dependencies better than RNNs. How does self-attention work? It computes attention scores between tokens using query, key, and value vectors. What is output of self-attention?

What is output of self-attention? A weighted combination of value vectors based on attention scores. What is multi-head attention? Multiple attention mechanisms running in parallel to capture different relationships. Why multi-head attention used?

Why multi-head attention used? It helps the model learn different types of patterns simultaneously. How multi-head attention works? Input is split into multiple heads, each computes attention separately, then combined. What is benefit of multiple heads?

What is benefit of multiple heads? Each head focuses on different semantic aspects of the input. What is final output? Concatenation of all head outputs followed by linear transformation. What is query (Q)?
What is query (Q)? Representation of the current token seeking relevant information. What is key (K)? Representation of tokens used to match against query. What is value (V)?
What is value (V)? Contains actual information passed after attention. Why Q, K, V needed? They allow flexible computation of attention scores. How Q, K, V generated?
How Q, K, V generated? Through linear transformations of input embeddings. What is scaled dot-product attention? Computes attention using QKT divided by sqrt(d). Why scaling used?
Why scaling used? Prevents large values that can destabilize softmax. What is output? Softmax(QKT  sqrt(d)) multiplied by V. Why softmax used?
Why softmax used? Converts scores into probability distribution. What does attention score represent? Importance of one token relative to another. What is positional encoding?

What is positional encoding? Technique to add order information to token embeddings. Why positional encoding needed? Transformers process tokens in parallel and lack sequence awareness. What is sinusoidal encoding?
What is sinusoidal encoding? Uses sine and cosine functions to represent positions. What is learned positional encoding? Position embeddings learned during training. What is benefit?
What is benefit? Helps model understand word order in sentences. What is masked attention? Prevents model from seeing future tokens during training. Why masking used?
Why masking used? Ensures autoregressive behavior in text generation. Where used? In decoder part of transformer. What problem solved?
What problem solved? Prevents information leakage from future tokens. How implemented? Using a mask matrix applied before softmax. What is encoder?
What is encoder? Part of transformer that processes input sequence into meaningful representations. What does encoder contain? Self-attention and feedforward layers. What is encoder output?
What is encoder output? Context-rich representations of input tokens. Where encoder used? In models like BERT. What is purpose?
What is purpose? Understand input context deeply. What is decoder? Part of transformer that generates output sequence. What layers in decoder?
What layers in decoder? Masked attention, encoder-decoder attention, feedforward. What is encoder-decoder attention? Decoder attends to encoder output. Where decoder used?
Where decoder used? In GPT and translation models. What is purpose? Generate tokens sequentially. What is feedforward layer?
What is feedforward layer? Fully connected neural network applied after attention. Why needed? Adds non-linearity and transformation power. How applied?
How applied? Independently to each token. What is structure? Two linear layers with activation function. What is benefit?
What is benefit? Enhances representation learning. What is residual connection? Adds input to output of a layer. Why used?
Why used? Prevents vanishing gradients. What is skip connection? Another name for residual connection. Where applied?
Where applied? After attention and feedforward layers. What is benefit? Improves deep network training. What is layer normalization?
What is layer normalization? Normalizes inputs across features. Why used? Stabilizes and speeds up training. Where applied?
Where applied? After each sub-layer. Difference from batch norm? Works per sample, not batch. What is benefit?
What is benefit? Improves convergence.

What is a vector database? - A specialized database designed to store and search embeddings (vectors) efficiently using similarity instead of exact matching. What is FAISS? - An open-source library by Meta for fast similarity search and clustering of dense vectors, widely used in local RAG systems. What is Pinecone?

What is Pinecone? - A fully managed cloud-based vector database that handles storage, scaling, and retrieval of embeddings without infrastructure management. What problem do vector databases solve? - They enable fast semantic search over large datasets where traditional databases fail. What is similarity search?

What is similarity search? - Finding data points whose embeddings are closest in meaning to a query vector. What is cosine similarity? - Measures similarity based on the angle between vectors, commonly used in vector databases. What is Euclidean distance?

What is Euclidean distance? - Measures straight-line distance between vectors, another way to compute similarity. What is dot product similarity? - Measures similarity based on vector multiplication, often used when vectors are normalized. What is indexing in vector DB?

What is indexing in vector DB? - A technique to organize vectors so similarity search becomes fast and scalable. What is brute-force search? - Comparing a query vector with every vector in the dataset, accurate but slow. What is Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN)?

What is Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN)? - A faster search method that sacrifices slight accuracy for speed. Why is ANN important? - It allows real-time search in millions or billions of vectors. What is nearest neighbor search?
What is nearest neighbor search? - Finding the most similar vectors to a given query. What is top-k search? - Retrieving the top k most similar vectors for a query. What is metadata in vector DB?

What is metadata in vector DB? - Additional information (like document ID, source) stored alongside vectors for filtering. Why is metadata filtering useful? - It allows narrowing search results based on conditions like date or category. What is hybrid search?

What is hybrid search? - Combining keyword search and vector similarity search. What is dense vector? - A vector with mostly non-zero values representing meaningful information. What is sparse vector?

What is sparse vector? - A vector with many zeros, typically used in keyword-based systems. Why vector DB instead of SQL? - SQL is not optimized for similarity search on high- dimensional data. What is embedding storage?

What is embedding storage? - Saving vector representations of text, images, or data in the database. What is vector dimension in DB? - The size of the embedding stored (e.g., 384, 768). Why must dimensions match?

Why must dimensions match? - Query and stored vectors must have same size for similarity calculations. What is FAISS index type? - Different structures like Flat, IVF, HNSW used to optimize search. What is Flat index in FAISS?
What is Flat index in FAISS? - Exact search method with high accuracy but slower speed. What is IVF index? - Clusters vectors into groups for faster approximate search. What is HNSW?
What is HNSW? - Graph-based index for very fast and accurate ANN search. What is quantization? - Reducing vector precision to save memory and speed up search. What is sharding in vector DB?

What is sharding in vector DB? - Splitting data across multiple nodes for scalability. What is replication? - Copying data across nodes for reliability and availability. What is latency in vector search?
What is latency in vector search? - Time taken to retrieve results. Why is low latency important? - Enables real-time applications like chatbots. What is recall in vector search?

What is recall in vector search? - Measure of how many relevant results are retrieved. What is precision in vector search? - Measure of how accurate the retrieved results are. What is tradeoff in ANN?
What is tradeoff in ANN? - Speed vs accuracy balance. What is batch insertion? - Adding multiple vectors at once to improve efficiency. What is real-time indexing?
What is real-time indexing? - Updating the index as new data arrives. What is vector update? - Modifying existing embeddings in the database. What is deletion in vector DB?
What is deletion in vector DB? - Removing vectors and updating the index accordingly. What is namespace in Pinecone? - Logical separation of data within the same index. Why use namespaces?
Why use namespaces? - To isolate datasets (e.g., different users or projects). What is scaling in Pinecone? - Automatically handling increased data and traffic. What is serverless vector DB?
What is serverless vector DB? - Managed DB where infrastructure is abstracted away. What is persistence in FAISS? - Saving index to disk for reuse. What is memory vs disk tradeoff?
What is memory vs disk tradeoff? - Memory is faster; disk is cheaper but slower. What is vector normalization? - Converting vectors to unit length for consistent similarity. What is query embedding?
What is query embedding? - Converting user query into vector before searching. What is document embedding? - Converting stored data into vectors. What is RAG  vector DB connection?

What is RAG  vector DB connection? - Vector DB retrieves relevant chunks for LLM to generate answers. What is chunk storage in vector DB? - Each document chunk stored as a separate vector. What is re-ranking?

What is re-ranking? - Reordering retrieved results using a more accurate model. What is cross-encoder re-ranking? - Using a model to compare query and results together for better accuracy. What is future of vector DB?
What is future of vector DB? - Core infrastructure for AI applications like search, chatbots, and recommendation systems.
